reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Equal access to high-quality education as a fundamental right; the documented value that early selection concentrates opportunity among high-SES pupils (KSH/Oktatási Hivatal admission statistics confirm strong skew); the principle that assessment design should not reinforce preparedness gaps created by family wealth.
reactions.interest: Low-SES and Roma pupils, particularly those in under-applying settlements; families with no educational capital to navigate selective admissions; children whose only path to secondary academic track currently runs through paid test-prep.
reactions.public_good_frame: Meritocratic access — talent and ability distributed across SES groups equally, but sorting begins at age 10 only when ability is truly independent of family resources. Reducing coaching-dependent sorting protects both equity (low-SES high-ability pupils) and social cohesion (reduces the perception that school choice is a mobility tool only for the wealthy).
reactions.argument: S1 directly addresses the mechanism that locks out our constituency: coaching-resistant assessment genuinely weakens the preparation-arms-race advantage, and reserved seats with funded outreach create affirmative pathways. The equity gain is conditional, not automatic — it requires (a) a functional SES proxy and (b) measurable uptake from funded outreach — but those conditions are testable. Critically: the scenario acknowledges that residual-skew and informal-selection risks exist; this honesty is important. However, two observations: first, the scenario's own evidence labels show that core mechanisms (coaching resistance, SES-proxy workability) are 'contested/moderate', not established. We cannot accept this if the SES proxy is gamed or if coaching reappears in interviews. Second, non-state maintainers' alignment is flagged as uncertain — if church schools absorb displaced selective demand without applying comparable rules, the net equity gain collapses. We support S1 only if: (i) the assessment is independently audited for coaching resistance before rollout, (ii) the SES-proxy gaming-rate is piloted and published before full deployment, (iii) non-state maintainers are bound by law (not 'negotiated voluntarily') to equivalent quotas, and (iv) outreach campaigns are evaluated for uptake, not just designed.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would shift to strong support if: (1) an independent audit of the redesigned assessment demonstrates <15% coaching-value-add compared to the current exam, published before rollout; (2) pilot SES-proxy data show gaming at <10% of applications; (3) legislative amendment mandates quota equivalence for all maintainers (including church/foundation schools), not voluntary alignment; (4) funded outreach reaches >70% of target low-SES/Roma settlements and generates measurable application-rate uplift (tracked via local education authority data). We would oppose if: (1) the assessment redesign is not independently validated; (2) non-state maintainers are not legally bound to equivalent rules; (3) the SES proxy is deployed without pilot gaming data; or (4) outreach is unfunded or unmeasured.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The documented principle that earlier tracking strengthens SES-outcome gradients (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006; Brunello & Checchi 2007 are cited); the equity value that delayed sorting preserves pathways for late bloomers and reduces the weight of family resources on educational trajectory. The documented fact (KSH/Oktatási Hivatal) that rural pupils are drained from local schools by early selective entry.
reactions.interest: Low-SES and rural pupils, particularly those whose talents emerge after age 10; families in rural settlements facing school network hollowing; Roma communities whose representation in selective schools is far below population share.
reactions.public_good_frame: Durable school networks — by retaining high-achievers in common schools until age 14, S2 protects rural school viability and keeps local secondary education visible as a pathway, not a fallback. Delayed sorting reduces the role of age-10 assessment in determining life trajectories, creating a second window for ability to emerge independent of early family investment.
reactions.argument: S2's logic is sound on paper: later sorting reduces SES-gradient sensitivity documented internationally. However, the scenario itself flags a mechanical and near-certain equity risk that neutralizes the benefit: as selective seats shrink, residual places become MORE SES-skewed (same applicant pool, fewer seats), absent an S1-type admission rule change. The scenario acknowledges this in equity_impact but offers no mechanism to address it. We face a choice: fewer, more-skewed selective seats (if no rule change) or capped selective seats plus reformed admission rules (S1+S2 combined). S2 alone, without S1's rule reforms, relocates rather than solves the sorting problem — it moves it to school choice within general schools and to non-state maintainers absorbing displaced demand. The political risks flag exactly this: non-state expansion is 'moderate' evidence, meaning plausible and documented. We support S2 only if: (i) it is bundled with S1-type admission reforms for the residual selective seats, (ii) non-state maintainer expansion is legally capped (not merely 'negotiated'), and (iii) general-school upper-grade capacity expansions are prioritized in rural settlements to prevent network collapse.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would shift to conditional support (not full) if: (1) S2 is explicitly paired with S1-style admission rule changes for residual selective places; (2) non-state maintainer intake is capped by law in line with state caps, with enforcement; (3) funding for general-school capacity expansion prioritizes rural settlements facing network hollowing (identified via tankerulet data); (4) a comparison-group evaluation tracks both high-achiever retention in common schools and non-state enrollment shifts by settlement type, published before year 5. We would oppose if: (1) S2 is implemented without rule reforms for residual selective seats (leaving them MORE skewed); (2) non-state caps are merely voluntary; (3) capacity expansion favors urban schools; or (4) the non-state absorption effect is not evaluated.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The documented principle from Pekkarinen, Uusitalo & Kerr (2009) and Jakubowski et al. (2016) that later tracking reduces SES-outcome gradients; the equity value that removing age-10/12 selection eliminates the entry point where family resources most strongly predict trajectory; the principle that comprehensive schools, coupled with teacher quality and early support, can equalize peer composition and reduce the within-school sorting that selective entry initiates.
reactions.interest: Low-SES and Roma pupils, particularly those whose abilities are not visible at age 10 due to unequal early learning; families with no educational capital to navigate selective admissions; rural communities whose schools are depleted by early selection; children whose only path to academic tracks currently depends on family wealth and paid test-prep.
reactions.public_good_frame: Equal citizenship in secondary education — no child's educational fate is sealed at age 10 by family background. Comprehensive schools to age 14, coupled with high-quality teaching and early diagnostics, make secondary education a common good rather than a sorting mechanism. This protects both equity (removes the primary lever by which SES predicts track) and social cohesion (delayed sorting reduces the perception that secondary education is a market good accessible only to the wealthy).
reactions.argument: S3 is the most direct attack on the sorting mechanism that locks out our constituency. The international evidence (Finland, Poland post-2010) shows that delayed tracking reduces SES-outcome gradients when bundled with teacher quality and early support — exactly the conditions S3 specifies. Removing age-10/12 selection entirely closes the entry point where paid test-prep and family cultural capital operate most powerfully. However, the scenario itself flags the largest equity risk: middle-class and non-state exit that relocates sorting rather than removes it. This risk is real and documented (school-choice literature; Polish precedent of private-school expansion). Our support is conditional on three things: First, the bundling is genuine — teacher retraining, early diagnostics, and reduced within-class sorting must be resources/legal mandates, not aspirations. The scenario's own uncertainty on teacher supply (flagged as contradicting the assumption) is the critical bottleneck. If teachers are not retained/retrained, comprehensive classes will stream informally, replicating the sorting we aim to eliminate. Second, non-state maintainers (church, foundation schools) must be extended the common-phase mandate by law — not 'negotiated' or left to voluntary compliance. The political risk flags middle-class flight; without legal extension, comprehensive reform becomes a 'public school only' policy, deepening the two-tier system. Third, evaluation must track sorting displacement — not just whether comprehensive schools reduce SES-outcome gradients in isolation, but whether the net effect across the whole system (comprehensive + non-state + school choice) actually narrows inequality, or merely relocates it. That evaluation is essential before rolling out beyond a comparison-group pilot. Strengths: S3 eliminates the mechanism (age-10 selection) most directly under our interest. Weaknesses: (1) the peer-effect channel is 'contested/weak' — we cannot assume within-school mixing alone closes the SES gradient without teacher quality; (2) teacher shortage is strong-evidence reality, not assumption — the scenario may be undeliverable; (3) non-state flight risk is evidenced but unresolved; (4) durability across electoral cycles is uncertain (Polish precedent shows reversal is possible).
reactions.condition_to_change: We would shift to strong support if: (1) Parliament legislates not just the common-phase mandate for state schools but extends it to church/foundation schools (binding, not voluntary); (2) Teacher retraining in differentiated instruction is a legal/budgetary mandate, not a best-practice goal, with union buy-in and workforce-exit mitigation; (3) Early diagnostic/remedial systems are established and funded before rollout, not pilot-dependent; (4) A comparison-group evaluation tracks sorting displacement across comprehensive + non-state + school-choice system (not just within comprehensive schools) and is published before national rollout. We would oppose if: (1) Non-state maintainers are not legally bound to common-phase rules; (2) Teacher retraining is unfunded or voluntary; (3) Evaluation is limited to comprehensive schools alone without tracking non-state/school-choice displacement; or (4) The reform is rolled out nationally without pilot evidence on sorting-displacement magnitude.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The documented principle that early sorting (age 10-12) concentrates SES-outcome gradients (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006; Jakubowski et al. 2016); the equity value that compensation without structural change is a band-aid, not a cure — it does not address the sorting mechanism, only its downstream effects. The fact (KSH/Oktatási Hivatal) that early selection locks low-SES pupils into non-selective tracks where peer composition and standards diverge.
reactions.interest: Low-SES and Roma pupils whose abilities may emerge after age 10 but are locked out by early selection; rural pupils drained by selective entry; families with no educational capital to navigate or challenge placement at age 10; the children whose only path to academic tracks is through paid test-prep.
reactions.public_good_frame: Structural fairness — compensation for unequal treatment is not a substitute for equal treatment. Targeted funding to disadvantaged general schools is necessary but cannot offset the 12-year penalty of early sorting. S4 legitimizes age-10/12 selection as permanent while asking disadvantaged schools to 'do more with less', which entrenches the two-tier system and shifts the burden of inequality from the sorting mechanism onto teachers and pupils in lower tracks.
reactions.argument: S4 is a rejection of the problem itself. The scenario acknowledges that it 'compensates downstream rather than altering the sorting mechanism' — that is precisely why we oppose it. Compensation does not work when the mechanism is age-10 selection at an age when ability is invisible to assessment independent of family resources. The scenario's own evidence status is 'moderate on the Portuguese package overall, but WEAK specifically on whether downstream compensation can offset sorting begun at ages 10-12.' That weakness is fatal. No country evidence exists for offsetting early selection at Hungary's age-10/12 selection age. Portugal did not select as early. The assumption that compensation can 'meaningfully offset' early sorting is unverifiable ex ante and contradicted by the structure of the problem: if a pupil is placed in a non-selective track at age 10, 8 more years of funding to their general school does not restore the 8 years of peer composition and curricular access they lost. Within-track improvement is possible; between-track mobility is not — the track walls are the problem. Second, S4 LEGITIMIZES early selection. Once compensation is in place, political pressure to reform the sorting mechanism disappears. Middle-class families see 'disadvantaged schools are now well-funded' and abandon any support for structural change. The scenario flags this as 'entrenchment risk, asserted rather than evidenced' — but entrenchment is the predicted political outcome. Targeted funding is always the first line cut in fiscal consolidation (strong evidence cited in the scenario). So the likely political trajectory is: S4 is adopted, targeted funding is cut within 3-5 years during fiscal consolidation, and early selection persists without even the compensation. Third, the scenario does not address rural school hollowing. S4 leaves selective entry intact, so high-achievers continue to be drained from rural schools, and targeted funding to remaining general schools does not restore the peer composition that made rural secondary education viable. The scenario's own political risks section admits that 'compensation may entrench rather than dismantle the sorting engine.' Strengths of S4: lower short-term structural disruption; does not require teacher retraining or legislative amendment; less politically contentious than S2/S3. Weaknesses: (1) addresses only the symptom (low attainment in disadvantaged general schools), not the disease (early sorting); (2) targeted funding is proven to be fiscally vulnerable and fragile across electoral cycles; (3) no evidence that compensation can offset age-10 selection; (4) entrenches the two-tier system and removes political pressure for structural reform; (5) does not address rural school network collapse.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would shift to conditional support (not full support, because the mechanism remains) if: (1) S4 is explicitly paired with S1-type admission rule reforms that weaken the SES skew of selective tracks — i.e., the two are implemented as a package; (2) targeted funding is legally ring-fenced for a minimum 15-year period (not vulnerable to fiscal consolidation cuts); (3) evaluation demonstrates that cohorts in disadvantaged general schools with the compensation package have within-school attainment gains AND measurable secondary-school-entry rates to academic tracks comparable to national averages (i.e., evidence that compensation reduces track-lock). We would continue to oppose S4 as a standalone scenario because: (1) it leaves age-10/12 selection intact, which is the root mechanism locking out our constituency; (2) no evidence exists that compensation can offset early sorting at this age; (3) targeted funding is politically fragile and historically cut first in fiscal consolidation; (4) it entrenches two-tier sorting while asking disadvantaged schools and pupils to absorb the structural inequality; (5) it does not address rural school network depletion.
